### Troubleshooting

 App won't start: Check antivirus settings, allow in security preferences
 AI chat not working: Verify OpenAI API key and internet connection
 Voice commands not working: Check microphone permissions
 Weather not working: Check internet connection

results.title: I made a Desktop Pet in a month, and he is silly!
results.content: noticed that as I was editing everything so there's nothing much I could do. Anyways this day I made a simple logic for the slimes states and placed them in game but well the state machine is still kind of broken for now since making an intermediate AI is not something I have learned so far but well this is the result of my first test. This day I fixed the state machine so they don't keep jumping non -stop and there's some type of decision making also made the system to render different colors for different slimes but well this system will probably be remade in the near future since I wasn't that happy with the final result once it wasn't easy to preview the final result of the slime with each color. So you know what I said yesterday yeah so I remade the system making it so you just [...] yesterday yeah so I remade the system making it so you just change the self modulate in each part with this it would be easier to save and load its colors. Also I was kind of tired from university that day so I just kept testing what I had while I was playing Pokemon TCG please I need help I am addicted. As you may have noticed on the previous videos my slimes or pets they only turned around when they hit the wall so I just improved the state machine and AI so they would turn around stop moving and jump kind of randomly with a decision making system. Friday day of sleeping late and working so good (irony) so I just started making every slime in the game it wasn't fun because it was really monotonous but well as you can see the workflow wasn't that bad too so I just spent the remaining [...] day trying to fix both these bugs and after a lot of time I finally fixed almost all the bugs related to it. I was kind of overwhelmed with the bugs that have been popping up so I shift my attention to UI once again so I hopped in asprite once again where I started making the shop UI and buttons so I could finally remove the prototype bad UI and that was about it today I applied the new UI to the game and as you can see everything is looking way better now with the new made UI I didn't had much time to work on the game today so that's just it for the 18 day I started working on the buying system and well it turned out really well at the first try that is a heresy so it was smooth and unstressed day I added the hide and unhide button if you don't want to be distracted by your pets but

results.title: Steam Community :: Desktop Pet Project
results.content:
Recommended

10.5 hrs on record

Posted: 2 November, 2023

it's alright, just your classic desktop pet program. its nice you can have multiple running at once, but maybe increasing the pet slot amount would be nice.  
  
The phone (which is basically a menu) you can use to summon items, play games etc. is cool. But I wish right clicking gave you interaction options with the pet itself, something other desktop pets I used before had. For example, instead of just summoning the phone, right clicking the cats would spawn an options menu that lets you pet the cat, make it sit and also open the phone.  
  
But i also think its cool that you can play games with the pet. Cool addition.

[GenyaDev

148 products in account](

2

7 people found this review helpful  
1 person found this review funny [...] At it's current stage, the game seems to be a little lacking in most departments. It doesn't have any sound effects for the pet; it doesn't make any sounds while it roams, eats or plays. There is a few different ways you can interact with the pet, such as a sleeping pillow, a basket ball and some different kinds of foods, definitely some that cats don't eat, which ruins the immersion for me. [...] Sadly due to all these things, I feel like the game lacks too much interactivity and longevity, and I feel like I can't really recommend the game. I wish steam had a middle way of voting for the game, as I feel like that would sum up my feelings on it currently the best. I do hope the developers will keep making the game better and including updates for it, and if that does happen I will keep you guys updated.

results.title: About desktop pets & virtual companions: discussing the inhabitants that fill the void of our digital spaces – The Candybox Blog
results.content:
Considering all these examples so far, I think it’s obvious that there isn’t just “one type” of desktop pet. I don’t think there necessarily is such a thing as a “bad” virtual companion either… Just a misplaced one.  
 For example, I think we didn’t like Clippy for how intrusive that was. Clippy’s intention wasn’t clear, and that didn’t fit with the feature set of the software it was part of. Therefore Clippy became a nuisance.  
 Every desktop pet is different, and all demand different levels of attention. When you design them, you have to communicate the type of attention it will require, and honor that. This so people know under what context they can incorporate that desktop pet while they are working, browsing the web, or whatever… [...] I get equated to Hypnospace Outlaw all the time now, on a level where people try to tell me that I’m unoriginal for “taking that style”.  
 So when Desktop Goose blew up in popularity, and people started saying that its dev is “bringing desktop pets back”, you can imagine that it stung a little. I got worried that the same would happen again. I’ve been making these since Neopets. It’s not like I’m a secret. It’s just that when men do the same thing it tends to count for more…  
 The dev DM’d me, completely out of the blue, and told me that he was contacting journalists to see if they can rectify to include me as the pioneer of this kind of desktop project, and asked if it was OK to tweet a supportive tweet of my work. [...] I would also like to point to the success of Neko Atsume as a reason that people really DO want virtual pets, and that these things (even the silliest and seemingly most mundane takes on virtual companions) can be ridiculously successful. I mean, try pitching a cat game where you can’t really interact with the cats, but you can only cat watch. It sounds like it wouldn’t work, but it does.  
 Virtual pets are still alive and well among indies today.  
 Maybe desktop pets (proper) are struggling, but there’s growing interest. I think that’s hopeful!

results.title: Tired of boring AI assistants? I built a "Desktop Pet" Copilot that wanders around your screen and writes code - DEV Community
results.content:
DEV Community

## DEV Community

Rain9

Posted on Apr 7

# Tired of boring AI assistants? I built a "Desktop Pet" Copilot that wanders around your screen and writes code

Hey everyone.

I don't know if you feel the same way when coding, but while current AI coding assistants are incredibly useful, they sometimes feel a bit clunky to use.  
You either have to constantly shift your focus back and forth between your code and the IDE sidebar, or context-switch to a browser to ask ChatGPT or Claude when you hit a general problem outside your specific project.

As a minimalist (read: lazy) developer, I kept thinking: How cool would it be if this AI could materialize on my screen like those old-school desktop pets (like Shimeji or BonziBuddy), wandering around and always on call? Algolia is the official search partner of DEV [...] Driven by this random thought over the holidays, I built an open-source desktop pet app — CodeWalkers.

## 🐶 What exactly is this?

Simply put, it's a cross-platform transparent desktop app built with Tauri V2.

It spawns a few pixel-art characters on your screen. These little guys don't just aimlessly wander over your code editor or browser—more importantly, they come with an "AI brain".

product

product

When you run into a coding issue, just click on a character, and a terminal panel pops up right there in place.   
Currently, I've wired them up to real backend brains: GitHub Copilot CLI and Gemini CLI. You can directly ask them how to write a regex, configure Nginx, or even explain a piece of code right in the popup panel.
